1:
The chapter opens with David standing on the threshing-floor and pronouncing it holy ground, and the witnesses gather first around the certainty of that word and then around the labour it set in motion. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the sentence with care — "This is a house of Jehovah, the true God, and this is an altar of burnt offering for Israel" — and reads it as resuming the narrative suspended at 1 Chron. 21:28, the place of the apparition being named "a house of God" just as Jacob had named Bethel in Gen. 28:17. For Ellicott the verse is the very goal of the whole account of the census and the pestilence, a story the Chronicler would otherwise have passed over, preserved only because it shows how the Temple site was fixed. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees that David spoke by inspiration — through the instinct and direction of God's Spirit, the same Spirit that gave him the pattern of the house in 1 Chron. 28:11 — so that "this is the place appointed by God for the building of his temple and altar."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the site itself into prophecy. It is fitting that God should choose his own ground, for all the earth is his; and the ground chosen was a Jebusite's threshing-floor — a happy presage, Henry thinks, of the gospel temple set up among the Gentiles, as Acts 15:16, 17 foretells. The church is God's floor, his threshing, the corn of his floor in Isa. 21:10, whose fan is in Christ's hand to purge it. The house is fixed here because the altar is here; temples were built for the sake of altars, and altars were long before temples. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sharpens the same claim into a rule of worship: until a temple is built, this place shall be reckoned the temple of God, and on this altar — not the one at Gibeon — Israel's burnt-offerings shall now be made; and he supposes David bought not only the threshing-floor but some adjoining ground, with Calmet, that there might be room for so great a building.

From the site the witnesses pass to the preparation. The reformed John Gill notes only that David spoke "within himself, or to some principal persons about him," but Henry unfolds the motive: David prepared abundantly before his death (v.5), quickened by his own approaching end, because two things pressed on him — that Solomon was young and tender and might lose time at the first, and that the house must be exceedingly magnificent, a type of Christ in whom all fulness dwells, and so "of fame and glory throughout all countries," the very grandeur inviting strangers to know the true God, as David foretold in Ps. 68:29. What our hands find to do for God, Henry urges, let us do with all our might before death, "for after death there is no device nor working."
2:
With the site fixed, David's first act is to muster a workforce, and the witnesses divide their attention between who these "strangers" were and why David used them. The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agree that they were proselytes — the sojourners of the Septuagint and Vulgate, admitted to dwell in the cities of Israel because they had submitted to the seven Noahic precepts and renounced idolatry. Gill gives two practical reasons for employing them: they were the better artificers, the Israelites being chiefly husbandmen and herdsmen, and freeborn Israelites ought not to be put to servile drudgery. Clarke adds that these strangers were set to the more laborious and difficult parts of the work, as at 1 Kings 9:21. But Gill will not leave the matter merely practical: he finds a mystery in it, that the Gentiles should have their share in building the spiritual house and church of God which the temple prefigured.

The grammatical-historical witnesses are more interested in identifying the men and their number. Keil and Delitzsch explain that the gerim were the descendants of the Canaanites whom Israel had not destroyed at the conquest but reduced to bondage, as recorded in 2 Chron. 8:7 and 1 Kings 9:20; their number was so great that Solomon could later set a hundred and fifty thousand of them to labour, as 1 Kings 5:15 reports. David appointed them to be hewers of squared stones, the "wrought stones" of 1 Kings 5:18. Charles Ellicott concurs that the strangers were the Canaanite population living on sufferance under Israelite dominion and liable to forced service, and he presses the detail that Solomon found them by census to number 153,600 souls, the census being a preliminary to apportioning their several tasks, as in 2 Chron. 2:17. He notes too that the word here rendered "gather together" is a late term, and that the whole notice does not say the work was begun at once, but only that the organisation of the serf-labour originated with David.

So the verse leaves the witnesses agreed on the fact and complementary in emphasis. Gill and Clarke draw out the theology and the courtesy of the arrangement — the Gentile stranger set to the temple's rough work, a shadow of the nations gathered into God's house, and the freeborn son spared the brick-kiln his fathers had known in Egypt. Keil, Delitzsch, and Ellicott anchor the same men in the hard facts of census and conscription, the Canaanite remnant bound to the labour their subjection required. Between them the picture is complete: the temple would rise on prepared stone, cut by hands David had numbered and set to stand, and the arrangement he began would be carried through, unaltered, by his son.
3:
The inventory of materials begins, and the witnesses treat David's iron and brass with a philologist's attention to the fittings of a great door. The reformed John Gill keeps to the plain sense: the iron was prepared in abundance to make the nails that would join the boards of the doors and gates, and to fasten their hinges. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fill in the joinery — the iron served partly for the pivots on which the folding-doors of the gates turned and partly to strengthen the boards of which the doors were made, while the "joinings" were properly iron cramps that held the stonework together; the brass, they add, was prepared in abundance and "without weight."

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, brings the ancient Near East to bear on every noun. The word for "nails," he observes, happens to occur only in the later books of the Old Testament but may well be an old word, comparable to the Assyrian asmarê, "spears," from the same root; the doors were the folding-doors of 1 Kings 6:34, and the "joinings" are literally things that couple or connect — iron clamps and hinges, the same term used of wooden braces in 2 Chron. 34:11. The "brass" is really bronze, much used in the ornamental work of ancient buildings, and here Ellicott points to the bronze plates that once adorned the doors of Shalmaneser's temple at Balawat, now in the British Museum, and to Sennacherib's boast that the doors of his palace at Nineveh were overlaid with shining bronze.

The one phrase that might strain belief — "without weight" — the witnesses take together and defuse. Ellicott calls it a natural hyperbole: the actual amounts would of course be known to the royal treasurers, just as the Assyrian records speak of spoils and captives "without number" and "without measure" when the scribes plainly kept their tallies. Keil and Delitzsch let the same phrase stand as a simple mark of profusion, the brass being beyond ready reckoning rather than beyond all counting. So the verse, small as it is, opens a window on the sheer scale of David's provision and on the craftsmanship the building would demand: not raw heaps only, but iron shaped to pivot and cramp and nail, bronze destined for gates that would shine like a king's, gathered in such quantity that the historian reaches for the language of the incalculable. The witnesses do not divide; they simply turn the bare list into a picture of a house whose very hinges were prepared long before its first stone was laid, and whose builder meant its doors to rival the proudest in the world.
4:
From metal the provision turns to timber, and the witnesses follow the cedar back to its Phoenician source. The reformed John Gill notes simply that the cedar trees were laid up in abundance to be sawn into boards and planks for the ceiling, the wainscotting, and the flooring of the temple. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the trade behind the timber: the men of Tyre and Sidon sent David cedar beams in abundance, probably in exchange for the grain, wine, and fruit that the Phoenicians drew from Israel, Sidonians and Tyrians being named together to denote the Phoenicians generally, as in Ezra 3:7. This informal commerce, they observe, was the forerunner of the formal treaty Solomon would later strike with Hiram king of Tyre for the delivery of the cedar in 1 Kings 5:15.

Charles Ellicott, the second grammatical-historical witness, hears in "cedar trees in abundance" another of the chapter's rhetorical flourishes — literally "beams of cedars without number," a deliberate exaggeration like the iron and brass "without weight" of the verse before. The Phoenicians, he agrees, brought the wood in the ordinary way of commerce, bartering it for the grain, wine, oil, and other produce of the soil that their own rocky coastland could not yield in sufficiency; and he too looks ahead to the day when Hiram would enter an express contract with Solomon to supply the cedar and the other materials for the building, as in 1 Kings 5:8.

The witnesses do not contend, and the very harmony of their reading is instructive. What David gathered by neighbourly barter, his son would secure by royal covenant; the same cedar that came down informally to the father would come by treaty to the son. Both the practical historians and the plain expositor see in the verse a quiet providence: the resources of the nations flowing toward Jerusalem before ever the temple rose, the trees of Lebanon felled for a house not yet begun. Gill fixes his eye on the finished panelling those beams would become — the fragrant cedar lining a house fit for the Name — while Keil, Delitzsch, and Ellicott set the timber in its economic and diplomatic frame, the exchange of Israel's harvests for the forests of the coast. Together they show David doing for the temple what he could: not building it, but heaping up its very substance, so that the magnificence he had spoken of would not want for the noblest wood the age could furnish, and the work his son inherited would begin already half-supplied.
5:
Now the reason for all this provision is spoken aloud, and the witnesses linger over the single word that carries it — Solomon "young and tender." The reformed John Gill reports Jarchi's guess that the prince was about twelve, though he notes the same word is used of Joshua when he was forty-two, and inclines himself to think Solomon was by now about twenty. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch reason more tightly that he cannot have been quite twenty, since he was born only after the Syro-Ammonite war of 2 Sam. 12:24 and still calls himself "a little child" at his accession in 1 Kings 3:7; the noun na'ar may of itself mean a grown youth, but the added word for "tender" holds it here to the boyish age. Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, presses the vagueness of the word from the opposite side: na'ar is even looser than the Latin adolescens, stretching from a new-born babe to a man in his prime, and the very same "young and tender" is applied to Rehoboam at the age of forty-one in 2 Chron. 13:7; "tender" he glosses as timid, the fearfulness of Deut. 20:8.

On the greatness of the house the witnesses are one. The building was to be "exceeding magnifical," literally to be made great upwards, "for a name and for a glory" to all lands; and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley gives the reason David could not scant it — it was intended for the honour of the great God, and was to be a type of Christ, in whom all fulness dwells and in whom are hid all treasures. Ellicott adds that David's "I will therefore make preparation" is less a bare resolve than an earnest desire and a self-encouragement to an arduous task, and cautions that we need not suppose the verse records any actual utterance; the historian is representing the king's motive, for "to say" in Hebrew often means simply to think.

Here Ellicott turns to defend the whole scene. It is "strange, but instructive," he remarks, that critics have been found so destitute of the historical faculty as to allege, with Gramberg, that the entire episode of David's preparations is a fiction of the Chronicler's, merely because the books of Samuel and Kings are silent upon it. The disagreements among the witnesses are therefore mild and internal — twelve years or twenty, a boy or a stripling — while their agreement is firm: a father, feeling his death approach, laid up abundance for a house too great for an inexperienced son to provision from nothing, and the silence of the older histories is no disproof of it. So David "prepared abundantly before his death," and the verse gathers site, materials, and motive into one resolve.
6:
David now summons his son to lay the charge upon him, and the witnesses read the summons as the hinge of the chapter. The reformed John Gill notes only that Solomon was brought before him, but the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw out the design: before his death David called Solomon to commit the temple-building to him and press it strongly upon him, first telling him that he had himself intended to build but had been forbidden. Their affectionate "my son," they observe, is fitting and even necessary to the address. Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, judges the whole speech that follows to be an ideal composition — a dying address shaped by the Chronicler, whose style runs through it — and notes that the stress falls on "the Lord God of Israel," the national aspect of the God for whom Solomon would undertake a national work.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the verse its fullest exposition, and within it raises a genuine question. David was forbidden to build because he had shed much blood (v.8); but what blood? Some, Henry reports, refer it to the blood of Uriah, a reproach that might well render him unworthy of the honour. Henry rejects this: the honour had been forbidden him before ever he shed that blood, so it must mean, as the text itself explains, the blood he shed in his many wars — shed justly and honourably in the service of God and Israel, yet enough to make him less fit for this work than another who had never been called to such bloody service. In laying David aside for this reason God showed how precious human life is to him, and intended a type of the one who would build the gospel temple not by destroying men's lives but by saving them, as in Luke 9:56.

Henry then gathers the positive reasons David presses on Solomon. God had designed him for it — "a son shall be born to thee... he shall build a house for my name" — and nothing is more powerful to engage us in any service than to know we are appointed to it. He would be a man of rest, with leisure and quiet for the work, for "where God gives rest he expects work." And God had promised to establish his kingdom, so that if Solomon would build up God's house, God would build up his throne. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the verse more soberly, taking "young and tender" to mean that Solomon was as yet without complete knowledge and experience, so that David must make all the preparation he could, the more since the house was to be so magnificent. The witnesses thus turn a bare summons into the heart of David's charge: a father debarred by his own bloodstained hands handing the peaceful work to a son chosen, rested, and promised.
7:
The charge proper begins with David's own longing — "it was in my mind to build a house unto the name of the LORD my God" — and the witnesses attend both to the text and to the tenderness of the address. The reformed John Gill marks only the setting, that David spoke this when Solomon was brought into his presence. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the opening of David's rehearsal of his own thwarted purpose: he had meant to build, but the Lord had not permitted the resolve, and now committed it to his son. They defend the reading "my son" over the received "his son," judging the affectionate word not only fitting but nearly required by what follows, and note that "it was with my heart" is a favourite idiom of the Chronicler for "I had intended."

Charles Ellicott, the second grammatical-historical voice, presses the same textual point with the manuscripts. Several Hebrew copies, the margin, and the Septuagint, Vulgate, and Targum rightly read "my son," the received "his son" being probably an oversight caught from "Solomon his son" in the verse before. "It was in my mind," he explains, is literally "it came near my heart" — a phrase common in the later books but rare in the older, recurring exactly in 1 Chron. 28:2 and paralleled in 1 Kings 8:17. To build "unto the name of the LORD" is to build where his name shall be, his real presence, as at 1 Kings 8:29; and the whole statement, Ellicott adds, looks back to the promise given through Nathan in 1 Chron. 17.

So the witnesses, few here, converge on a single movement of the heart. David's ambition had been the noblest a king could form — to give the ark a house worthy of the Name — and the verse is careful to record that the ambition was holy and that its refusal was God's, not any failure in the wish. Keil, Delitzsch, and Ellicott alike keep the reader's eye on the word "my son," as though the whole weight of the transfer lay in that one syllable of intimacy: what the father had purposed and been denied, he now lays, not as a burden but as an inheritance, upon the child of his own house. The longing did not die when it was forbidden; it was handed down. And the ground of the transfer, that David's heart was right though his hand was barred, prepares the reader for the divine reason the next verse will give — that the man after God's own heart was still, for this one work, the wrong man.
8:
Here the divine refusal is spoken, and the witnesses openly divide over why the shedding of blood should bar the man of God's own heart from building God's house. The reformed John Gill notes plainly that "the word of the LORD came to me" means the word of prophecy, delivered, as the Targum says, by the mouth of Nathan. But the reason itself the witnesses read very differently. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke takes it as a matter of ritual fitness: heathens, Jews, and Christians alike, he says, have agreed that soldiers should have nothing to do with divine offices, for the shedding of human blood ill comports with the benevolence of God or the spirit of the Gospel; and he illustrates it at length from Virgil, where Aeneas, fresh from slaughter and red with battle, dares not handle the household gods until he has washed in a living stream. On this reading the bar is a kind of defilement, real though incurred in lawful war.

The other wesleyan-arminian voice, John Wesley, refuses that construction. It is "not that wars are simply unlawful," he insists; rather the prohibition is a type — the church, of which the temple was an illustrious figure, was to be built by Christ the Prince of Peace of Isa. 9:6, gathered and built up not by might nor by power but by God's Spirit, as Zech. 4:6 declares, and by the preaching of the gospel of peace. David was merely "less fit for that service than one who had not been called to such bloody work," and by setting him aside God showed how precious human life is to him. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott complicates both readings. The oracle here only partly matches Nathan's word in 1 Chron. 17, which promised David success in war without a hint that warfare would unfit him; and in 1 Kings 5:3 Solomon implies rather that David's wars had left him no leisure to build. The emphatic word, Ellicott shows, is "blood" — "torrents of blood hast thou shed earthward before me" — language that may echo Gen. 9:5 and the prophets' cries against men of blood, such as Amos 1:3 and the vengeance threatened on the house of Jehu in Hos. 1:4, and which recalls David's own hard usage of the Ammonites in 1 Chron. 20:3; or it may simply express the interpretation David's own conscience put upon the oracle. So the question stands genuinely open among them — whether the blood-bar is a ritual defilement, a typological fitness, or merely the want of a peaceful reign's leisure — and the witnesses leave it, as the text does, without dissolving the mystery of a holy war that still stained the hands that waged it.
9:
Against the man of blood the oracle now sets the man of rest, and the witnesses dwell on the name that is itself a prophecy. The reformed John Gill observes that the promise "a son shall be born to thee" was given to David before Solomon's birth, a word spoken over an unborn child. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws out the play on the name: Solomon, Shelomoh, comes from the root shalam, "he was peaceable," and so, alluding to the name, the Lord promises "I will give peace, shalom, in his days."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott unfolds the philology and the contrast at length. "Who shall be a man of rest," he explains, is set deliberately against "a man of war" such as David had been, and is filled out by what follows — "I will give him rest from all his enemies round about," the surrounding peoples, subdued under David's yoke, acquiescing in the son's dominion; the same words are used of David himself in 2 Sam. 7:1 and applied by Solomon to himself in 1 Kings 5:4, and the peace they promise is the peace of Prov. 16:7, where a man's enemies are at peace with him. On the name itself Ellicott is rich: the Hebrew Shelomoh keeps in its cognates — the Assyrian Shalman, the Moabite Salamanu — the final n that the Greek Salomon and our Solomon preserve, and the Vulgate renders it Pacificus, the peace-maker. It would seem, he notes from 2 Sam. 12:25, that the child's God-given name was really Jedidiah, "beloved of the Lord," but posterity, looking back with fond regret to the palmy days of his reign, remembered him only as Shelomoh, "the Peaceful." The "quietness" promised with the peace, he adds, is a word found only here, cognate to the "rest" of Judg. 5:31.

The witnesses do not divide; they build a single portrait. The very name of the coming king is made a divine augury of the character of his reign, so that the house of God should be raised not amid the clash of David's campaigns but in the settled quiet of a son whose name meant peace. Gill fixes the wonder that the word came before the birth — the child named and destined while yet unconceived; Clarke hears the pun that binds name to gift; Ellicott traces the name across three languages and back to Jedidiah, the beloved who became the peaceable. Between them the theology is plain: rest is God's gift and God's design, and it is into rest, not into war, that the building of his house is fitted. The man of blood prepares; the man of peace builds.
10:
The oracle reaches its summit in the sonship and the throne, and though only two witnesses linger here, they open the covenant that the verse compresses. The reformed John Gill glosses "he shall build an house for my name" as building it for the worship of God, and for his honour and glory — the whole end of the temple gathered into that one phrase, "for my name." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the verse in its larger frame, noting that it repeats the promise first given through Nathan in 1 Chron. 17, and that parts of the charge in the next verses are here anticipated and repeated.

For the verse is not one promise but three interlocked. "He shall build an house for my name" is followed by "he shall be my son, and I will be his father," and that by "I will establish the throne of his kingdom over Israel for ever" — the temple, the divine sonship, and the everlasting throne bound in a single breath. Ellicott's cross-reference to 1 Chron. 17 is deliberate, for it is there that the Lord had told David his seed would build the house and that God would be to him a father and he to God a son, the very covenant now laid before Solomon as the ground of his charge. Gill's insistence that the house is "for my name" keeps the reader from mistaking the building for a monument to Solomon: it is God's name that is to dwell there, God's worship that is to be served, God's honour that the magnificence is meant to display.

The witnesses do not contend, and their brevity is itself a kind of reverence before the weight of the words. What David hands his son is not merely a construction project but a place in the covenant — the son who builds the house is the son whom God owns as his own, and the throne he is promised is not for a generation but "for ever." Later hands would feel the tension between that "for ever" and the actual after-course of the dynasty, which Ellicott himself will raise at the charge to obey; but here the promise stands unqualified, the high-water mark of David's charge. The peaceable son, named before his birth and rested by God's gift, is now told that the house he will raise is bound up with a sonship and a kingdom that outrun his own life. The verse thus lifts the whole chapter from architecture to covenant: to build for the Name is to stand within the promise made to the fathers, and to be, in building, the son of God's own choosing.
11:
With the promise spoken, David turns from prophecy to blessing — "Now, my son, the LORD be with thee" — and the two witnesses weigh whether the words are a prayer or a pledge. The reformed John Gill hears in them both: "the Lord be with thee," or, as some render it, "shall be with thee," and if it be taken as a prayer it was no doubt a prayer of faith; he notes the Targum's paraphrase, "may the Word of the Lord be thine help." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the clause as a wish rather than a command — "mayest thou prosper and build the house" — the verb "prosper" meaning to carry a thing through to success, as it is used transitively in 2 Chron. 7:11 and Gen. 24:40.

Ellicott is struck too by the familiar cadence of the greeting. "The Lord be with thee" is the very benediction spoken over those of whom it is said "the Lord was with him," as in 1 Sam. 3:19 and 2 Kings 18:7; and this phrase, he remarks, is the origin of the familiar liturgical formula, "The Lord be with you." The closing words, "as he hath said of thee," Ellicott shows to use an idiom reserved for divine threats and promises — the same construction as "the word of the Lord became upon me" in the earlier verse — binding David's wish to the sure oracle already given, so that the blessing is not a father's fond hope only but a claim upon a promise God has spoken.

So the verse holds prayer and promise in a single breath, and the witnesses keep both. Gill will not decide too sharply between petition and prediction, content that faith may read it either way; Ellicott ties the wish firmly to the word already sworn, so that David prays for exactly what God has pledged. The effect is to make the blessing a model of believing intercession: David asks that Solomon prosper and build, but he asks it "as he hath said of thee," pleading the promise back to the God who made it. There is nothing anxious in the charge here; the old king, near his end, lays his hand of blessing on the son and calls down upon him the one thing needful — the presence of the Lord, without which the greatest preparations would be vain, and with which even a young and tender builder could not fail. The liturgy of the later church, Ellicott's note reminds us, still carries the echo of this deathbed benediction, "The Lord be with thee," now spoken over every gathered people as it was first spoken over the builder of the house.
12:
The blessing narrows to a single, supreme request — wisdom — and the witnesses mark how David subordinates every other gift to the keeping of the law. The reformed John Gill reads "the Lord give thee wisdom and understanding" as asked both for the management of so weighty an affair and for the government of the people. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the force of the opening word: "only the Lord give thee wisdom" means rather "at least may the Lord give," restricting the wish to the one supremely important point, the wisdom that Solomon would afterward receive in 1 Kings 3:9. "Give thee charge concerning Israel" he renders "appoint thee over Israel," as in 2 Sam. 7:11; and though Solomon had already been marked as David's successor, the father's prayer for God's own ratification of that appointment was by no means superfluous, unless the son were somehow exempt from the common human liability to err.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fasten on the condition that closes the verse — "that thou mayest keep the law of the LORD thy God" — and read it as the true hinge of the whole charge: the obtaining of the promised result hangs upon the faithful observing of God's commands. The speech, they note, is filled with reminiscences of the law, echoing Deut. 7:11 and its like, so that David's prayer for his son's wisdom is at the same time a summons to obedience. Ellicott hears the same continuative turn in the grammar, "and mayest thou keep," a favourite construction of the Chronicler that makes law-keeping the very purpose of the wisdom sought.

The witnesses converge on a striking order of priorities. Of all that a king might crave — wealth, victory, long life — David asks for his son first and chiefly the wisdom to govern and the grace to obey; and even the throne's divine appointment, already promised, is made the matter of earnest prayer rather than taken for granted. Gill keeps the two objects of that wisdom in view, the building and the governing; Keil and Delitzsch make obedience the condition of all success; Ellicott ties the wisdom and the law-keeping into one grammatical breath. Together they show that the magnificence of the coming house is not, in David's mind, the deepest thing at stake. A temple may be built by prepared stone and hired craftsmen; but a kingdom kept for God requires a king who keeps God's law, and it is for that — for a wise and obedient heart — that the dying father prays. Solomon's own later petition for "an understanding heart" would prove, the reader may sense, an echo of this very prayer offered over him before he reigned.
13:
The charge now states its condition plainly — "then shalt thou prosper, if thou takest heed to fulfil the statutes" — and the witnesses draw out both the Deuteronomic ring of the words and the sober truth they carry. The reformed John Gill observes that the same things are said here as were said elsewhere to Solomon, a repetition that shows the words were spoken by David a little before his death. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott makes the condition emphatic: the verse leaves it quite clear that obedience was an indispensable condition of the full realising of the promise, so that the unconditional-sounding "for ever" of the earlier verse must be read alongside the actual after-course of history.

Ellicott hears Deuteronomy throughout. "If thou takest heed to fulfil the statutes and judgments" is a reminiscence of that book, as at Deut. 7:11; "be strong, and of good courage" he would render more bluntly, "be stout and staunch," a frequent phrase in Joshua 1:7; and "dread not, nor be dismayed" echoes Deut. 1:21. The word "dismayed," he notes, means literally "broken" — broken in spirit, metu fractus — and answers pointedly to the "young and tender," the timid youth, of the earlier verse: the very fearfulness David had confessed in his son is the thing the charge now braces against.

Yet Ellicott will not let the conditional darken into despair. The word of the Lord, he insists, does not return unto him void; and though the earthly dynasty of David did in time come to an end through disobedience, in due season was born an heir of David and of Solomon who is at this day the Lord of a spiritual dominion that will endure throughout the ages. So the verse looks two ways at once. Read against Solomon and his sons, its "if" is a warning that was tragically fulfilled: prosperity was tied to obedience, and disobedience forfeited the throne. Read against the greater Son, its promise stands unbroken, the kingdom established for ever in the heir who kept the law his fathers broke. Gill's small chronological note — that David spoke thus near his death — gives the words their pathos: a dying man bracing a fearful son with the old Mosaic summons to courage and obedience, knowing that the house and the throne alike would stand or fall not by the weight of the gold he had heaped up, but by whether his son would take heed to do the statutes of the Lord. Be strong, be staunch, and keep the law — this is the spine of the whole charge.
14:
Now David lays open the sheer scale of what he has amassed, and the witnesses divide sharply over how to take the staggering figures — a hundred thousand talents of gold and a thousand thousand of silver. First they settle the opening phrase. The reformed John Gill reads "in my trouble" of the affliction that dogged the greater part of his reign, wars abroad and rebellions at home; but the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott agree that the word means rather "by my painful labour" or "by my toil," not "in my poverty" as the Septuagint and Vulgate render it, comparing "the labour of my hands" in Gen. 31:42. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads it in the same practical key — David curtailed his own expenses and lived in comparative poverty that he might save all he could for the building.

On the numbers themselves the schools part company. Clarke takes them at face value and does the arithmetic, reckoning the gold at over five hundred million pounds and the silver at more than three hundred and fifty million, some eight hundred and sixty-one million pounds in all — a real hoard, gathered from David's many conquests. The other wesleyan-arminian voice, John Wesley, defends the figures as possible against the objection that so much gold would have built the whole temple of solid metal: not all of it, he answers, was spent on the fabric, but a great part on the near two hundred thousand workmen and much laid up in the sacred treasures, the talent being three thousand shekels as in Exod. 38:24. But the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry balks: expounding the charge, he calls the sum incredible, and reports that most interpreters either allow an error in the copy or take "talent" to mean no more than a plate or ingot, himself inclining to think a certain number is put for an uncertain, since the next verse says the gold and silver were "without number." Ellicott presses this furthest: the figures are "incredibly large," round numbers in thousands, vivid Oriental exaggerations meant to suggest extraordinary magnitude rather than to be pressed literally — as when Ramses in the poem of Pentaur boasts of slaying thirty thousand bulls, or Scripture speaks of thousands in Dan. 7:10 and Mic. 6:7; the very symmetry of the figures, he adds, argues against mere textual corruption. So the debate is threefold and unresolved — literal treasure, corrupted or loosely-used numerals, or deliberate hyperbole — while all agree on David's closing hint, "thou mayest add thereto," which Clarke calls a gentle but pointed spur, and which Solomon took, adding to the stores of timber and stone in 2 Chron. 2:3.
15:
From treasure David turns to the hands that will spend it, and the two witnesses dwell on the abundance and the skill of the workforce he has gathered. The reformed John Gill notes simply that all the strangers of the land, mustered at David's order, stood ready with Solomon in abundance, men skilled in every manner of work. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch expand the trades named in the verse: the "hewers," who in the earlier verse were specifically stone-cutters, are here joined with "workers of stone and timber," so that the word embraces both masons and carpenters; and "all manner of cunning men for every manner of work" denotes not mere labourers but craftsmen of thorough mastery and skill in their several kinds. These are the very workmen, they add, whom Solomon would afterward acknowledge in his message to Hiram, as recorded in 2 Chron. 2:6.

The verse, brief as it is, completes the tally of David's provision. He had named the site, heaped up the metals and the timber, weighed out the incalculable treasure; now he points to the labour force already levied and standing by — not raw hands but experienced artificers, hewers and masons and carpenters and master-craftsmen, the whole apparatus of a great building assembled in advance. Gill's stress on "in abundance" and Keil and Delitzsch's stress on "cunning" together answer the anxiety David had confessed over a young and tender son: Solomon would not have to recruit or train his workforce from nothing, but would inherit a body of skilled men ready to begin.

There is a quiet argument in the arrangement of the charge. Having pressed obedience and courage upon his son, and having shown him the mountains of gold and silver and cedar, David closes by reminding him that even the skill to work them is provided. Nothing that human foresight could supply has been left wanting. And yet the very next words will not let Solomon rest in the sufficiency of stone and craftsman: "Arise, and be doing, and the Lord be with thee." The workmen are ready, the materials are ready, but the work still waits on the man; abundance of means is not the same as the doing of the thing. So the verse, which might seem a mere logistical note, in fact sets up the exhortation that follows — that all this lavish preparation is offered not to excuse Solomon from labour but to leave him without excuse, that he should rise and build the house for which his father had furnished everything but the finishing hand.
16:
The catalogue of gold, silver, brass, and iron ends not in a sum but in a summons — "there is no number; arise therefore, and be doing" — and the witnesses read the turn from provision to command. The reformed John Gill takes "of the gold, the silver, the brass, and the iron" to describe the cunning artificers, some skilful to work in gold and silver, others in brass and iron, so that the metals name the crafts as well as the materials. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch group all the metals together here as the main thing, the repeated particle bringing each once more into prominence, and render the climax, "As for the gold, it cannot be numbered — arise and be doing, and Jahve be with thee."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the very phrase "arise therefore, and be doing" recurs at Ezra 10:4, a summons to a sacred task; and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the exhortation into a lesson for every reader. "Be doing," he says — when thou shalt come to the throne; for the sense of God's presence must not slacken our endeavours, but rather quicken them: because he is with us, we must rise and be doing, and then he will be with us even to the end. "Work out your salvation," Wesley adds, borrowing the apostle's word, "and God will work in you."

So the witnesses let the verse pivot the whole charge from the passive to the active voice. Everything countable has been counted and found beyond counting; the materials are "without number," the craftsmen ready, the treasure heaped past reckoning — and precisely there, where a lesser man might sit down satisfied in the abundance, the word comes, "Arise." Gill's craftsmen and Keil and Delitzsch's incalculable gold are the ground; Ellicott's parallel and Wesley's application are the point. The blessing "the Lord be with thee" is not offered as a substitute for labour but as its spur: divine presence and human diligence are yoked, not opposed. This is the second time in the charge that David couples the promise of God's presence with the command to rise and work, and Wesley's gloss catches the paradox at its heart — that the surest sign of trusting the God who is with us is not to fold the hands in the confidence that he will do it all, but to rise and be doing, assured that our labour in such a cause shall not be in vain. The preparations were finished; the doing had only begun.
17:
The charge widens from the son to the nation's leaders, and the witnesses read David's turn to the princes as the enlisting of a whole court behind a young king's work. The reformed John Gill identifies them plainly as David's courtiers and nobles, the great men about him. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes a small textual point — the word "saying" is absent from the Hebrew, an omission paralleled in 1 Chron. 16:7 and elsewhere — but the weight of the verse falls on the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry's reading of why the princes were summoned at all.

Those on the throne, Henry observes, cannot do the good they would unless those about the throne set in with them; and so David would have the princes advise Solomon, quicken him, and make the work as easy to him as they could, every one promoting it in his own place. He presses two things upon them. First, their obligation: in gratitude for the great things God had done for them — giving them victory, rest, and a good land for an inheritance — they were bound to be zealous, "for the more God has done for us the more we should study to do for him." Second, the disposition that alone would carry it: they must "set their heart and soul to seek God," placing their happiness in his favour and keeping their eye on his glory, making religion their choice and business; for let the heart be sincerely engaged for God, and the head and hand, the estate and interest, will all be cheerfully employed for him.

Henry then lifts the princes' task into a word for every later worker. "Whatever is done towards rendering the word of God generally known and attended to," he says, "is like bringing a stone, or an ingot of gold, towards erecting the temple." This should hearten us when we grieve that we see little fruit of our labours, for much good may appear after our death which we never thought of; and so "let us not be weary of well doing." The work, he reminds us, is in the hands of the Prince of Peace, its Author and Finisher, who is pleased to employ us as his instruments, that we should arise and be doing, encouraging and helping one another, working by his rule and in dependence on his grace, assured that our labour shall not be in vain in the Lord. The witnesses do not divide; Gill names the men, Ellicott fixes the text, and Henry turns David's political appeal to his nobles into a summons that reaches every servant of God who lends a hand, however hidden, to the building of his house.
18:
David grounds his appeal to the princes in a question that is really a confession of grace — "Is not the LORD your God with you? and hath he not given you rest on every side?" — and the witnesses trace both the rest and the conquest that made the question rhetorical. The reformed John Gill reads the divine presence concretely, as God's blessing them with wealth and riches. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott expound the two clauses that answer the question: the Lord had given Israel rest round about, and had given the inhabitants of the land into David's hand, the land being subdued before the Lord and before his people. The "inhabitants," Keil and Delitzsch are careful to say, are not the Israelites but the Canaanites still left in the land and the other enemies David had overcome; and Ellicott identifies these surrounding peoples with those whose reduction is described in 1 Chron. 18 through 20, the language of a subdued land recalling Num. 32:22 and Josh. 18:1, and the giving into the hand echoing Josh. 2:24. Clarke adds the Targum's softening of the opening question — "Is not the Word of the Lord your God your assistant?" — and observes that David, now king not only of Judah but master of most of the surrounding nations, was handing Solomon every possible advantage.

And here the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke turns aside into a lament that outruns the verse. Solomon, he reflects, came to the throne with every advantage, and had he made proper use of his state and his talents would have been the greatest as well as the wisest of sovereigns; "but alas! how soon did this pure gold become dim!" He traces the decline in a single mournful sentence — the unlawful marriage that led to a forbidden commerce, the multiplied heathen wives that turned his heart from God, until "the once wise and holy Solomon died a fool and an idolater." It is a striking intrusion of the end of the story into its bright beginning, the ruin foreseen at the very hour of the charge.

The witnesses do not contend over the verse itself; its meaning is plain, that the God who had given rest and victory would surely be with the work of his own house. What Clarke's digression supplies is the shadow. David appeals to an unbroken record of grace — presence, rest, a subdued land, wealth without stint — as the ground on which the princes and the prince should build; and the reader, warned by Clarke, knows how that lavish beginning would be squandered. The rest was real, the advantages real, the presence real; but rest and riches, the sequel would show, are no security where the heart turns away. For the moment, though, the question hangs unanswered because it needs no answer: the Lord their God was with them, and the time to build had come.
19:
The chapter closes as it began, with a summons to build, but now the outward work is rooted in an inward one — "set your heart and your soul to seek the LORD your God" — and the two witnesses hold the two together. The reformed John Gill reads the seeking as the heart's whole engagement for God's honour and glory, most sincerely and cordially and to the utmost of one's power. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes the exact construction — "to seek unto the LORD," as in 2 Chron. 17:4 and Ezra 4:2, a later idiom than the simple accusative of 1 Chron. 16:12 — and then draws the decisive link between the seeking and the building. "Arise therefore, and build," he explains, is better "and arise ye, and build," the second clause explaining how the first was to be carried out: to build the Lord a fair and noble sanctuary was itself the appointed way of seeking his favour.

Ellicott presses the cost of that seeking. Professions cost nothing, he observes; but they were not to serve the Lord "without cost," as David had refused to do at the threshing-floor in 1 Chron. 21:24. The end of the whole labour, he adds, was to bring the ark of the covenant up from its temporary abode on Mount Zion, where it had been lodged since 1 Chron. 15:1, together with the holy vessels of God, into the house that was to be built for the Name — the participle "to be built" being the same form used of the son who "shall be born" earlier in the charge.

So the two voices seal the chapter on a single note: the temple is not an end in itself but the destined home of the ark and the vessels, and the building of it is the outward shape of a heart set to seek God. Gill guards the inwardness — the soul cordially engaged — lest the great work sink into mere masonry; Ellicott guards the outwardness — the actual rising and building, the costly sanctuary — lest the seeking evaporate into profession. Between them the charge to the princes, and through them the whole book's account of David's last labour, comes to rest: seek the Lord with heart and soul, and prove the seeking by arising to build. The ark that had wandered and lodged in tents was to have a house at last, and the way to that house lay through hearts wholly given to the God whose presence it was made to hold. With that the old king's charge is finished, and the building he could not raise is left, fully furnished and fully commanded, in the hands of the son and the princes he had stirred to the work.
